story:
Bill recognizes the shape behind his stained-glass door: the outline of a uniformed figure, a knock that sounds like authority. He coaches himself to act natural. The face in the mirror looks calm enough—older, thin, blue-eyed—nothing that betrays the panic moving under his skin.

He opens the door to find not a stranger, but Officer Jake Kitchener, a neighbor he’s grilled burgers with and knows too well. That familiarity makes the visit worse.

“Morning,” Jake says. The word is friendly, but his posture isn’t. Official now. Stiff.

“Hey,” Bill manages.

“Can I come in a minute?”

Bill steps aside. Jake enters, his eyes doing what cops’ eyes do—taking inventory without seeming to. The unwashed dishes. The ring of water in the sink. The half-wiped counter.

“Is Norma home?” Jake asks.

Bill doesn’t miss a beat. “Left early. Church thing.” He gestures at the sink as if it explains everything. “She rushed. That’s why—”

Jake gives a small nod like he’s heard a hundred versions of the same excuse.

“I’m looking for a girl,” he says. “Christa Buchanan. Seventeen. Tall, real skinny. Dyed black hair. Tattoos.”

Bill feels the name land in him like a dropped tool.

“She’s missing,” Jake continues. “Kids saw her in the neighborhood last night, during the storm. They said she was high—probably meth. With the rain and the dark, her parents are scared somebody offered her a ride, or she got coaxed into a car. Could’ve been anyone.”

As Jake talks, Bill’s gaze flicks—not to Jake, not to the door, but down the hall where the laundry room sits half-closed. He can still see last night’s wet footprints in his head, rainwater traced with something darker. The clothes he’d shoved into the washer because Christa told him to, because she didn’t want blood on her hands or his. He imagines the smell leaking out, imagines a red thread creeping across tile toward Jake’s boots.

He forces his face to stay blank.

Jake asks whether Bill noticed anything unusual—strange cars, people wandering, yelling in the rain.

“No,” Bill says. “I… I didn’t see anything.” He swallows and reaches for the one truth he can safely hand over. “I was working late in the garage.”

Jake watches him for a beat. The pause is small, but Bill feels it like a hand on the back of his neck.

“What kind of work?”

“Fixing a cabinet. Tools. Just—noise.” Bill gives a shrug that feels rehearsed even as he does it. “Didn’t go outside.”

That part is true. He had been in the garage all night—shaking, crying, moving only when the gun told him to move—after the shot that dropped Norma where she stood. He had dragged his wife out of sight under the press of a barrel against his spine. And then, with that same gun trained on him, he had done what a grieving husband should never have to imagine doing. Not because he wanted to. Because survival has a voice, and last night it sounded like a teenage girl whispering, Do it, or I’ll do worse.

Jake’s eyes pass over the kitchen again, then settle back on Bill’s face.

“Okay,” Jake says at last, as if deciding something. “If you hear anything, if Norma hears anything—have her call me. Just in case.”

“Of course,” Bill says. He manages a friendly, worried smile, like any decent neighbor would.

Jake leaves. The door clicks shut.

Bill stands there for one full breath, then two, listening for footsteps on the porch, for the cruiser door, for the engine. When the sound finally fades, his knees loosen and he half-falls against the wall, the relief ugly and immediate.

“He believed me,” he whispers—not toward the laundry room, but toward the hall closet, the one he’s been avoiding looking at straight-on. “He’s gone. Now stop aiming that thing at me.”

The closet door opens.

Christa steps out, unfolding from the dark like she’s been poured from it. She’s tall, like Jake said—long-limbed and bony—but she looks smaller in Bill’s unbuttoned dress shirt, swallowed by fabric, hair clinging in damp black strands around her face. Young. Even fragile.

Until you see the gun.

It’s the same gun that sparkled with rain when she appeared at the door last night. The gun that barked when Norma was too slow telling her where the liquor was. The gun that prodded Bill down the hall and into obedience. The gun that turned a decent man into someone who could answer a cop’s questions without blinking.

Outside, Sunday is bright and quiet, the storm rinsed away like it never happened.

Inside, Bill keeps breathing because the muzzle says he must.

Jake’s questions circle back through Bill’s head—Christa Buchanan, missing, high, anyone could’ve pulled over—and the truth of the room makes them obscene. Christa was never far. She’s right here: a scared, strung-out teenager holding a weapon she shouldn’t have, and Bill—terrified, obedient—has learned to act natural to stay alive.

The gun has taught him.
